title: Building a Survey & Polling Platform in Laravel
subtitle: Questions as data, response collection, aggregation pipelines, anonymity guarantees, branching logic, and real-time results — the Laravel architecture behind a survey and polling platform.

sections.h2: How a survey & polling platform fits together

blocks.p: A survey platform is a data-driven instrument that collects answers, enforces participation rules, and aggregates results. The pressure: creators define questions (not developers), so every question type, branch rule, and anonymity setting is data; the same engine must render the instrument, validate responses, prevent duplicate votes, and compute live or exportable aggregates.

blocks.diagram:
Creator (researcher / admin)
 └─ Survey (title, status, anonymity mode, close_at)
     ├─ Questions (type, options, required, branch rules, position)
     ├─ Responses (answers keyed by question id + respondent token)
     └─ Aggregates (counts, percentages, weighted scores)
          └─ dashboards, exports, real-time broadcasts
 Respondent ──► answers questions ──► validated + deduped ──► stored

blocks.p: The defining action — a response — flows through validation, identity/anonymity checks, and aggregation:

blocks.diagram:
Respondent submits answers
   │
   ▼ load survey schema (questions + branch rules + status)
   ▼ resolve visible questions from branch logic
   ▼ build validation rules dynamically from schema
   ▼ check duplicate vote / quota / survey closed
   ▼ validate ; on fail → errors keyed by question
   │ on pass
   ▼ store Response (answers + respondent_token hash)
   ▼ update aggregate counters (sync or queued)
   ▼ broadcast SurveyResultsUpdated (Reverb/Pusher) if live results enabled

ul: Question schema — questions as data (type, options, required, branch rules, scoring weights).
Response collection — validate against schema, enforce one-vote rules, handle partial vs complete submissions.
Aggregation — precomputed counters vs on-demand queries; how anonymity affects storage.
Branching & live results — skip logic re-evaluated server-side; cached aggregates pushed over websockets.

blocks.callout: A survey platform is a runtime interpreter of a question schema plus an aggregation pipeline: one schema drives rendering, validation, and reporting. The hard parts are trustworthy server-side branching, duplicate-vote prevention, and fast aggregates without leaking identity.
blocks.title: The mental model in one sentence

blocks.p: Both collect answers from a schema, but they differ in scope and rules. A poll is typically short (one or few questions), often public, with immediate tallies and strict one-vote enforcement. A survey is longer, may be multi-page, supports branching, quotas, and partial completion. In Laravel, model both as the same Survey with a kind flag or shared tables — the engine is identical; UX and validation rules differ (e.g. polls reject partial saves).

Each question is a record: type (single_choice, multiple_choice, likert, text, ranking, matrix), prompt, options (for choices), required, branch_rules, and optional weight for scoring. Store questions as ordered rows or JSON. The application interprets the schema; it never hard-codes a specific instrument.

Three common modes. Anonymous: store no user id or PII; use a hashed respondent_token (cookie/session fingerprint) only to prevent duplicate votes — never reversible to identity. Identified: link responses to user_id for longitudinal studies. Confidential: store user id separately from answers in different tables with strict access controls so analysts can't join them casually. Document the mode in the survey record; enforce it in storage and export APIs.

Polls require at-most-once participation. Layer checks: authenticated users → unique index on (survey_id, user_id); anonymous → hash a stable token (signed cookie + survey salt) and unique index on (survey_id, respondent_token_hash). Rate-limit by IP as a backstop, not the sole guard (shared NAT). Return a friendly 'already voted' without revealing whether others voted.

For live polls, precomputed counters win: on each response, increment choice counts in an aggregate_options table or Redis hash — O(1) reads for dashboards. For complex surveys (cross-tabs, filters, open-text search), query normalized answers on demand or maintain a reporting projection. Never expose per-response rows in public live results; only publish totals.

Questions can show/skip based on earlier answers ('if satisfaction < 3, show follow-up'). Store rules as data referencing question ids. The client uses them for UX, but the server must re-evaluate during validation — only require/accept questions that should be visible given submitted answers. Skipped questions must not appear in required validation.

When show_live_results is enabled, push aggregate updates after each response via Laravel broadcasting (SurveyResultsUpdated on a public channel keyed by survey slug). Cache the latest totals in Redis; the broadcast payload carries counts only, never individual answers. Disable or delay live results until the poll closes if early votes would bias participation (bandwagon effect).

Surveys move through draft → open → closed. Respect close_at, max response count, and per-segment quotas (e.g. stop accepting when 100 responses from '18–24' are reached). Check status and quota inside a transaction before insert to avoid race overshoot.

blocks.callout: Treating a poll's 'one vote per person' as client-side only, or storing anonymous tokens in plain text, are the classic survey-platform failures. Enforce participation rules and anonymity on the server with hashed tokens and database constraints.
blocks.title: Most common misconception

blocks.p: A survey platform stores questions as a schema, collects validated responses with server-enforced anonymity and duplicate-vote rules, maintains aggregate counters for fast reporting, re-evaluates branching on the server, and optionally broadcasts live totals without leaking individual answers.

blocks.h4: Analogy — the ballot box and tally board

blocks.p: The ballot is the question schema; the slot accepts one envelope per voter (duplicate prevention). Anonymous surveys use a numbered stub torn off and hashed — you can tell if someone already voted, but not who they are. The tally board on the wall shows running totals (precomputed aggregates), updated after each ballot is counted. Branching is like conditional pages on a paper form ('if you answered No, skip to question 5') — and the official counter re-checks those rules, not the voter.

blocks.h4: Diagram — schema drives collection and aggregation

blocks.diagram:
┌─────────────────┐
           │ Question Schema │ (questions as data)
           └────────┬────────┘
        ┌────────────┼────────────┐
        ▼            ▼            ▼
     Render     Validate     Aggregate
   (UI/flow)   (server +     (counters /
               branch logic)   broadcasts)

blocks.p: Each lesson is one idea. Code illustrative.

blocks.h3: Lesson 3 — Build validation from visible questions

items.q: Q1. How do a poll and a survey differ in this architecture?
items.a: Same engine (question schema + responses + aggregates); polls are short, often public, with strict one-vote rules and immediate tallies. Surveys are longer, may allow partial completion, branching, and quotas. Model both with shared tables; differ by kind/status rules.

items.q: Q2. What three anonymity modes exist and how do they affect storage?
items.a: Anonymous: no user_id/PII, only hashed respondent_token for dedup. Identified: link response to user_id. Confidential: separate identity from answers with strict access controls. The survey record declares the mode; storage and exports must enforce it.

items.q: Q3. How do you prevent duplicate votes in an anonymous poll?
items.a: Issue a stable token (signed cookie), hash it with a survey-specific salt, store only the hash, and enforce a unique index on (survey_id, respondent_token_hash). Rate-limit by IP as backstop. Never rely on client-side checks alone.

items.q: Q4. Why precompute aggregate counters for live results?
items.a: O(1) reads for dashboards and websocket payloads; querying all responses per page view doesn't scale. Increment counters on each response (DB or Redis), broadcast totals only — never individual rows.

items.q: Q5. How must branching (skip) logic be handled on the server?
items.a: Re-evaluate visibility from submitted answers before validation; only require/accept questions that should show. Client branching is UX only — hidden questions must not be required or stored as answered.

items.q: Q6. What checks run before accepting a response?
items.a: Survey not closed (status/close_at), quota not exceeded, duplicate vote not detected, visible questions resolved, dynamic validation passes — ideally inside a transaction to avoid race overshoot on quotas.

blocks.p: Re-do the quiz from memory; redraw 'schema → validate → aggregate' each session.

head: When
Session (~10–15 min)
Goal
rows: Read packet; quiz with answers open.
Quiz closed-book; restate response submission flow.
Interleaved quiz; write rulesFor() from blank.
Closed-book interleaved pass; misses restart Day 1.

blocks.callout: Retrieving just before you'd forget produces the strongest gain.
blocks.title: Why the gaps grow

blocks.p: Topics shuffled to train choosing the right idea.

items.q: M1. Live poll totals lag 30 seconds behind submissions. Likely cause?
items.a: Aggregates updated in a slow queued job or queried on-read instead of incrementing counters. Increment precomputed counters synchronously (or fast queue) and cache latest totals for broadcast.

items.q: M2. An 'anonymous' survey exports user emails alongside answers. Fix?
items.a: Anonymity mode violated — anonymous surveys must not store or join PII. Separate confidential identity if needed; enforce mode in schema, storage, and export APIs.

items.q: M3. Users report voting twice from the same phone on a public poll. Bug?
items.a: Duplicate prevention missing or client-only. Add hashed respondent_token with unique DB constraint; authenticated polls also unique on (survey_id, user_id).

items.q: M4. A follow-up question is required even when the branch should skip it. Bug?
items.a: Branch logic not re-evaluated server-side. Compute visible questions from answers before building validation rules.

items.q: M5. Early voters skew results because everyone sees live percentages. Mitigation?
items.a: Disable live results until close, or show totals without percentages, or delay broadcast — bandwagon bias is a product rule, not just a tech issue.

items.q: M6. Quota for 'Region: West' exceeded by 3 responses under concurrent load. Fix?
items.a: Check quota inside a transaction with row lock or atomic counter decrement; reject when quota reached. Race without transaction allows overshoot.

ul: Build duplicate prevention two ways — IP-only vs hashed token + unique index — and watch IP-only fail on shared networks.
Break anonymity on purpose — store raw cookies or join user_id to answers in an 'anonymous' survey, then audit exports.
Write bypass tests first: second vote, hidden-question injection, closed survey submit, quota race.
Add multi-page surveys with partial saves, resume tokens, and progress percentage.
Stretch goals: cross-tab reporting, weighted scoring (NPS), survey logic builder UI, CSV/SPSS export, quota segments, scheduled open/close, and bias-aware live-result modes.

flashcards: What a survey platform fundamentally is
A runtime interpreter of a question schema plus an aggregation pipeline for collection, validation, and reporting.
Survey vs poll in one line
Same engine; polls are short/public/one-vote/live-tally; surveys are longer with branching, quotas, partial saves.
Three anonymity modes
Anonymous (hashed token only), Identified (user_id linked), Confidential (identity separated with strict access).
Anonymous duplicate-vote pattern
Stable cookie token → HMAC hash with survey salt → unique (survey_id, respondent_token_hash).
Why not IP-only dedup
Shared NAT/college networks cause false blocks or missed duplicates; token hash is primary.
Precomputed vs query-on-read aggregates
Counters: O(1) live reads. Query: flexible but slow at scale. Use counters for polls, projections for complex surveys.
Server-side branching rule
Re-evaluate visible questions from submitted answers; only validate/require those.
Live results broadcast rule
Push totals/percentages only via SurveyResultsUpdated; never individual response rows.
Survey lifecycle states
draft → open → closed; enforce close_at, max responses, and segment quotas before insert.
Quota race fix
Check/decrement quota inside a DB transaction with locking to prevent overshoot.
Bandwagon bias mitigation
Hide live percentages until close, or delay/disable live results during voting.
Question types to model as data
single_choice, multiple_choice, likert, text, ranking, matrix — each with options and validation rules.
Confidential vs anonymous
Confidential may store identity separately for admin use; anonymous must never allow casual re-identification.
glossary: Survey schema
The set of questions (as data) defining an instrument.
Question
A typed prompt definition: type, options, required, branch rules, weight.
Poll
A short, often public survey with strict one-vote rules and live tallies.
Response
A respondent's set of answers to a survey, stored keyed by question id.
Respondent token
A stable client identifier hashed for anonymous duplicate-vote prevention.
Anonymity mode
Whether responses link to identity: anonymous, identified, or confidential.
Branching logic
Rules showing or skipping questions based on earlier answers.
Aggregate counters
Precomputed per-option counts updated on each response for fast reads.
Quota
A cap on responses for a survey or segment (e.g. demographic bucket).
Live results
Real-time broadcast of aggregate totals, not individual answers.
Skip logic
Server-re-evaluated rules determining which questions are visible.
Reporting projection
Denormalized answer store derived from responses for analytics/export.
Bandwagon effect
Participation bias when voters see early results before casting their vote.
